text: Isn't it funny how history just keeps
repeating itself? Here we are in the
year of our Lord 2025 with a new
Fantastic 4 movie on the way from a
troubled studio already blighted by
rumors of a difficult production and
desperate lastminute re-shoots to try to
salvage the movie. And for some reason,
it brought back memories of another
Fantastic 4 movie from 10 years ago. a
movie with such a nightmarishly
difficult production and such
disastrously bad reception that it
basically killed the studios involvement
with the franchise and the director's
entire career. I'm talking of course
about Fantic.
Oh my god.
Now, to fully understand how this movie
came about, I need to give you a little
history lesson. The problems date all
the way back to the 1980s when a cash
strap Marvel sold off the film rights to
some of their most iconic characters in
a decision that still haunts them to
this very day. In the case of the
Fantastic 4, the rights went to
Constantine Films in 1986, but they came
with a 10-year time limit, basically
meaning that the studio had to do
something with them by 1996 or they'd
default back to Marvel. Unfortunately,
the '90s weren't exactly a golden age
for superhero flicks. There was no real
audience demand for a Fantastic 4 movie
and Constantine Films didn't have the
resources to do something like that
anyway. So, they did what any [ __ ]
studio would do and hired the king of be
movies himself, Roger Corman, to direct
a low-budget unreleased Fantastic 4
movie purely as a rights retainer.
Apparently, though, the actors were
never told about this plan and thought
that they were making a genuine movie.
But whatever. The first serious attempt
came in 2005 when the superhero craze
finally took off. 20th Century Fox
bought the rights and made their own big
budget adaptation of the Fantastic 4 and
it was yeah, okay, I guess it made the
studio a decent profit and it was enough
to greenlight a sequel, but Rise of the
Silver Surfer cost more and made less
and it was a pretty [ __ ] movie overall.
So, plans for a third movie were quickly
scrapped. But you don't need me to tell
you that Marvel had not been idle during
this time. The MCU launched in 2008 and
quickly turned into a box office
juggernaut. Marvel, who were once forced
to sell off the rights to their own
characters just to keep the lights on,
were the new king of the hill, and they
wanted their [ __ ] toys back. That old
licensing agreement from way back in
1986 was still in effect. And if Fox
didn't release a new movie by 2016, the
rights to the Fantastic 4 would revert
to Marvel again. Clearly, this was not
acceptable, and so a new Fantastic 4
movie was green lit in 2009 with Akiva
Goldsman producing and Michael Green
writing the scripts. The plan was to
reboot the franchise and start over,
ignoring the first two movies and
hopefully launching a new franchise.
Unfortunately, they struggled to make
much progress for the first few years.
But that all changed with the release of
Chronicle, a low-budget dark sci-fi
superhero movie by Josh Tran that went
on to make $120 million against a 15
million budget. In the process, Trunk
was catapulted from a relatively unknown
newbie to Hollywood's Golden Boy
overnight, and Fox were quick to see his
potential at the helm of a Fantastic 4
movie. So, pretty soon, Tran was hired
to direct with Jeremy Slater doing the
script writing. Unfortunately, this is
the point in our story where things
start to go wrong. According to Slater
himself, the two men had completely
different creative visions for this
film. Slater wanted a bright,
optimistic, and very conventional
superhero movie. Basically, Fox's answer
to the Avengers. Trank, on the other
hand, wanted a dark, gritty
psychological story about personal
trauma and body horror. You know, kind
of like Chronicle. Slater also claimed
that Tran had no interest in the
Fantastic 4 or superhero movies in
general, rejected most of his ideas,
rewrote the script himself, and left him
out of discussions with the studio. The
two men were at loggerheads right from
the start, and eventually all became too
much for Slater, who left the project
after about 6 months. With time running
out, Simon Kinberg was brought in to try
to salvage something from Slater's
notes. What they ended up with was a
Frankenstein's monster of a script
stitched together from different ideas
that still didn't have a finished third
act. But [ __ ] it. There was no choice
but to press ahead and hope they could
figure something out later. Casting also
became a big point of contention.
Allegedly, Tran wanted Allison Williams
for the role of Sue Storm, but Fox
overruled him and cast Kate Mara
instead. A decision that would come back
to haunt all of them once shooting
began. He did get his own way with Miles
Teller and Michael B. Jordan, though,
even if it meant rewriting the script to
make him Sue's adopted brother instead
of her actual brother because, well, you
know. Anyway, shooting finally got
underway in May 2014. And by all
accounts, the problems began almost
immediately. According to this article
from the Hollywood Reporter, Tran began
micromanaging his actors to the point of
obsession. During takes, he would be
telling cast members when to blink and
when to breathe. One person says he kept
pushing them to make the performance as
flat as possible. One particular target
for him was apparently Kate Mara.
According to an interview she did with
the Independent in 2020, she described
the experience as horrendous, that she
felt she was singled out because she was
the only woman on an allmale set and
claimed that she was reduced to tears on
multiple occasions. One popular theory
is that Tran was still pissed about
having his casting choice for the
character overruled by the studio and
deliberately set out to sabotage Mara's
performance. Tran, on the other hand,
said that these claims were either
blatant mistruths or extreme
exaggerations to the point of satire.
I'll let you be the judge. But it seems
like Kate wasn't the only one having
issues with him. Miles Teller was
getting increasingly frustrated with
Tran's directing style and clashed with
him on several occasions. At one point,
things apparently even got physical
between the two men. Again, Tran offered
a different explanation and claimed that
Teller was arrogant and full of himself.
An interview with Polygon says the actor
took a I'm a movie star now approach to
acting, which involved questioning even
the most low impact performance
requests. Either way, there's no denying
that the atmosphere on set was getting
increasingly toxic as the shoot dragged
on. And according to reports from
multiple sources, Tran began to isolate
himself from the rest of the cast and
crew. He holed up in a tent and cut
himself off from everybody, says one
highlevel source. He built a black tent
around his monitor. He was extremely
withdrawn between setups. He would go to
his trailer and he wouldn't interact
with anybody. Tran later claimed that it
was just a traditional video village
setup because sometimes you can't
actually be out standing next to the
camera because the camera's on a [ __ ]
dolly. Needless to say, Fox were getting
pretty nervous about all of this, but
they were also reluctant to intervene
for two reasons. One, the movie was
halfway through production, and it would
be tough to find anyone willing to take
over. And two, they already had a
reputation for micromanaging big film
projects at the time, and they didn't
want to make things even worse. So, they
waited until the first rough cut was
ready. And wow, did things go downhill
fast after that. Up until then, there
was still some kind of hope that Tran
could deliver on his creative vision for
the film. Sure, there had been problems
on set, but that wasn't exactly unknown
in the film industry. But after that
first rough cut, there was just no
hiding it anymore. Fant forstick was
turning into a [ __ ] disaster.
Straight away they went into panic mode
and ordered major re-shoots, especially
on the third act. Tran was sidelined and
Simon Kinberg took over trying to cobble
together an ending from the scraps that
they had. Getting the cast back together
was almost impossible since they'd all
moved on to new projects by that point.
So, a lot of material was shot with
doubles and the production moved to LA
to film scenes with Teller against a
green screen. It was chaos, says a crew
member, adding that Tran was still in
attendance, but was neutralized by
committee. Kate Mara had shaved her head
for another role, forcing the production
to shove the kind of wig on her that
would make Steven Seagal feel
embarrassed and just hope that nobody
would notice. Well, it turns out that
they did. Fanforstick finally released
into the August dumping ground in 2015
to a critical lambastion and went on to
bring in less than 170 million
worldwide, officially making it a flop.
Plans for future movies were immediately
cancelled, and Josh Tran did his best to
distance himself from the movie, feeling
like it was no longer even his project.
It was basically a gigantic
embarrassment for everyone involved. And
clearly, someone had to carry the can.
So, naturally, Fox wasted no time
throwing Josh Tran under the bus. Before
you knew it, conveniently timed articles
were starting to pop up in the trades,
detailing the problems on set and
blaming Tran for most of it. Almost
overnight, he went from Hollywood's
golden boy who was being touted as the
next J.J. Abrams. Remember when that was
considered a compliment to a virtual
pariah in the industry that nobody
wanted to work with? In fact, in the
decades since this movie came out, he's
only released one film. But was it
really all his fault or was he just a
misunderstood scapegoat to hide a much
bigger problem? Well, I think in
retrospect, he was definitely the wrong
choice for the project and his creative
vision didn't really mesh with the tone
of the Fantastic 4. He was also an
inexperienced director thrown into a
project 10 times bigger than anything
he'd worked on before and expected to
just magically make it happen. And to be
fair, there's been some defense of him
in recent years. According to one crew
member who worked on the movie, the film
was illconceived, made for the wrong
reasons, and there was no vision behind
the property. Say what you will about
Marvel, but they have a vision. Are you
serious? Oh, how times have changed.
Another commented, "To me it was a
classic indictment of the entire system.
Give Josh Tran a 20 million movie. Groom
him. But they don't make those movies
anymore. Nobody should escape scrutiny
on this one. Everyone should take a good
look in the mirror. Myself included.
Even I probably did the movie for the
wrong reasons. But hey, I'm sure
everyone learned their lessons when it
came to Fantastic 4 movies. I mean, it's
not as if a disaster like this could
ever happen
again,
right? Anyway, that's all I've got for
today. Go away now.
